content.full_text:
PARKER, MILLIKEN, CLARK, O'HARA & SAMUELIAN
A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION
ATTORNEYS AT LAW

SACRAMENTO OFFICE:
CAPITOL PLACE
915 L STREET, SUITE 1180
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
TELEPHONE (916) 443-0500
FACSIMILE (916) 443-8156

333 SOUTH HOPE STREET, 27TH FLOOR
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90071-1488

TELEPHONE (213) 683-6500

CLAUDE L. PARKER (1877-1952)
JOHN B. MILLIKEN (1893-1981)
RALPH KOHLMEIER (1903-1976)

FACSIMILE (213) 683-6669

WRITERS' DIRECT DIAL NUMBER:
(213) 683-6598

FRANK ALBINO

September 1, 1992

Fair Political Practices Commission
428 "J" Street
Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95812

Attention: Scott Hallabrin, Esq.,
General Counsel

Re: Request for Interpretive Opinion re Milton Marks
Post Government Employment Restrictions Act of 1990

Gentlemen:

This letter requests your legal opinion as to whether and to what extent I may represent physicians in connection with licensing disciplinary proceedings before the Medical Board of California ("the MBC") without violating the Milton Marks Post Government Employment Restrictions Act of 1990 (Government Code §§ 87406 et seq., hereinafter, "the Marks Act") or other applicable provisions of law, if any.

Facts

I am an attorney-at-law licensed to practice in California and a former member of the Division of Medical Quality ("the DMQ") of the MBC. My term of office on the MBC expired on July 31, 1992.

The DMQ is the division of the MBC that enforces the California Medical Practice Act (Business and Professions Code §§ 2000 et seq.). As such, the DMQ has the power to revoke, suspend or otherwise discipline the licenses of California physicians who violate that act and makes quasi-judicial decisions in licensing disciplinary cases against

PARKER, MILLIKEN, CLARK, O'HARA & SAMUELIAN
ATTORNEYS AT LAW

Fair Political Practices Commission
September 1, 1992
Page 2

physicians. Procedurally, MBC disciplinary proceedings against physicians typically are
conducted as follows:

1. After investigation by MBC investigators and a determination
by the MBC's Chief of Enforcement (made, as legally required, without DMQ
involvement or knowledge) that cause for disciplinary action against a physician
exists, a formal Accusation against the physician is filed by the MBC's Executive
Director.

2. If a proceeding commenced by the filing of an Accusation is
not settled, it is referred for initial hearing either to a Medical Quality Review
Committee (an "MQRC") or, more often, to an Administrative Law Judge (an
"ALJ") of the Office of Administrative Hearings. MQRC's are entities under the
MBC that are provided for in Article 13 of Chapter 5 of Division 2 (§§ 2320 et
seq.) of the Business and Professions Code.

3. The MQRC or ALJ then conducts an quasi-judicial
administrative hearing pursuant to the Administrative Procedure Act in which the
complainant (the MBC'S Executive Director) is represented by the Attorney
General's office and the charged physician is represented by private counsel. At the
conclusion of the administrative hearing, the MQRC or ALJ makes findings of fact
and conclusions of law and issues a Proposed Decision in the matter.

4. The Proposed Decision is transmitted to the DMQ for review
and adoption or nonadoption.

5. If the DMQ fails to nonadopt the Proposed Decision within 90
days, it automatically becomes a final decision that is appealable to the Superior
Court.

6. If the DMQ votes to nonadopt the Proposed Decision within
90 days, the DMQ must review the transcript of the administrative hearing and
make its own decision in the matter. In connection therewith, the parties are given
an opportunity to present written and oral arguments, based upon the record,
before the DMQ. The DMQ then makes a decision, which, once again, is
appealable to the Superior Court.

I would like to be able to represent physicians who have been charged with
violations of the Medical Practice Act in connection with MBC disciplinary proceedings of
the type described above.

PARKER, MILLIKEN, CLARK, O'HARA & SAMUELIAN
ATTORNEYS AT LAW

Fair Political Practices Commission
September 1, 1992
Page 3

Law

It is my understanding that, as a former member of the MBC, my ability
under law to represent physicians in connection with MBC disciplinary proceedings is
subject to the provisions of the Marks Act. In this regard, Government Code Section
87406(d)(1) provides, in pertinent part, that:

"No designated employee of a state administrative agency,...for a period of
one year after leaving office or employment shall, for compensation, act as
agent or attorney for, or otherwise represent, any other person by making
any formal or informal appearance, or by making any oral or written
communication, before any state administrative agency...for which he or she
worked or represented during the 12 months before leaving office or
employment, if the appearance or communication is made for the purpose of
influencing administrative action, or influencing any action or proceeding
involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of
a...license....For purposes of this paragraph, an appearance before a State
administrative agency does not include an appearance...before an
administrative law judge...."

In addition, there may be other laws applicable to this situation of which I am not aware.

Questions

With reference to all of the foregoing, I would appreciate your opinion with
respect to the following questions pertaining to my ability under law to represent physicians
charged with violations of the Medical Practice Act in MBC licensing disciplinary
proceedings during the one-year period following the termination of my employment as a
member of the MBC, i.e., during the period from August 1, 1992 through July 31, 1993:

1. I interpret the "administrative law judge" exception in § 87406(d)(1)
mean that I may lawfully represent a physician charged by the MBC with a violation of the
Medical Practice Act in an administrative hearing on that charge before an ALJ. However
would this exception also allow me to represent the physician in administrative hearing
before an MQRC?

2. Between the time an Accusation is filed against a physician and an
administrative hearing takes place on that Accusation, there will typically be
communications, discovery and, often, settlement negotiations between the attorney

PARKER, MILLIKEN, CLARK, O'HARA & SAMUELIAN
ATTORNEYS AT LAW

Fair Political Practices Commission
September 1, 1992
Page 4

representing the physician and the Attorney General's office, as counsel for the MBC.
Would the "oral or written communication" language of § 87406(d)(1), or any other
provision of law, prevent me from engaging in such communications, discovery and
settlement negotiations with the Attorney General's office prior to the administrative
hearing? Would the answer to that question depend on whether the hearing was to be
held before an ALJ or an MQRC? In answering these questions, you should be aware that
any proposed stipulation settling an MBC disciplinary case must be approved by the DMQ.

3.      In some instances, a physician whose case has been investigated by the
MBC may be asked to stipulate to licensing discipline before a formal Accusation is filed.
Would I be able lawfully to participate in negotiations with MBC enforcement staff and/or
the Attorney General's office with regard to such a proposed stipulation?

4.      Would any restrictions on my ability under law to represent physicians
in connection with MBC disciplinary matters affect my law firm (of which I am a 3%
shareholder-employee) or any other shareholder- or nonshareholder-attorney employed by
my law firm? In particular, assuming that I could not, myself, lawfully present written or
oral arguments to the DMQ in connection with a disciplinary proceeding in which I
represented the physician in question before an ALJ and the DMQ nonadopted the
Proposed Decision, could another attorney in my firm present such arguments? If so,
could I assist that person in the preparation of such arguments if I did not actually appear
before the DMQ myself?

      All of the foregoing questions assume that any physician whom I represent in
connection with an MBC licensing disciplinary proceeding would be one in whose
proceeding I did not "participate," within the meaning of Government Code § 87406(d),
while I was a member of the MBC, as I am aware of the prohibitions of § 87401 in this
regard.

      As I am in need of urgent guidance on these questions, I would appreciate a
response to this letter at your earliest possible convenience.

Thank you for your assistance.

Sincerely yours,

[signature]

Frank Albino
